theory.intro: By taking a sample, recording it, and returning it before the next draw (**sampling with replacement**), you can estimate the makeup of a whole collection you cannot see.
facts: The **sample proportion** is $\dfrac{\text{number of that colour}}{\text{number of draws}}$.
Estimate of a colour in the whole $= \text{sample proportion} \times \text{total in collection}$.
Replacing each draw keeps the proportions the same for every draw.
Larger samples give estimates closer to the true makeup.
steps: Write the sample proportion for the colour as a fraction.
Multiply that proportion by the total size of the collection.
Round sensibly — the answer is an estimate, not exact.

foundation.q: In $10$ draws (with replacement) from a bag, $5$ were red. What fraction of draws were red?
A sample of $10$ marbles (with replacement) had $3$ blue. Estimate $P(\text{blue})$.
In $20$ draws, $10$ were red. Estimate the fraction that are red.
In $20$ draws, $5$ were red. A bag holds $60$ counters. Estimate how many are red.
In $10$ draws, $2$ were green. A bag holds $50$ counters. Estimate the number green.
In $10$ draws, $4$ were blue. What fraction of draws were blue?
In $20$ draws, $15$ were red. A jar holds $40$ marbles. Estimate the number red.
A sample of $10$ cards (with replacement) had $6$ red. Estimate $P(\text{red})$ as a decimal.
In $10$ draws, $1$ was yellow. A bag holds $30$ counters. Estimate the number yellow.
In $20$ draws, $8$ were blue. What fraction of draws were blue?

development.q: In $25$ draws, $10$ were red. A bag holds $100$ counters. Estimate the number red.
In $50$ draws, $30$ were blue. A tub holds $200$ counters. Estimate the number blue.
A sample of $40$ draws had $10$ green. A box holds $80$ counters. Estimate the number green.
In $20$ draws, $12$ were red and $8$ were blue. Estimate the number of red in a jar of $50$.
Cards drawn with replacement $30$ times gave $18$ picture cards. A deck has $40$ cards. Estimate the number of picture cards.
In $50$ draws, $35$ were red. Estimate $P(\text{red})$ and the number red in a bag of $20$.
A spinner of unknown make-up landed red $9$ of $30$ spins. If it has $10$ equal sectors, estimate how many are red.
In $40$ draws, $24$ were blue, $16$ were red. Estimate the number red in a tub of $100$.
Over $20$ days the weather was rainy on $6$. Estimate how many rainy days to expect in a $30$-day month.
A sample of $25$ marbles had $5$ yellow. Estimate the number yellow in a jar of $200$.

mastery.q: A bag of $80$ counters is sampled $40$ times (with replacement): $14$ red, $26$ blue. Estimate the number of each colour.
In $50$ draws, $20$ were red. A student estimates $40$ red in a bag of $100$. A second sample of $200$ gives $30\%$ red. Which estimate is more reliable?
Marbles drawn with replacement $60$ times: $36$ red. Estimate the number red in a jar of $150$.
A sample of $30$ draws gave $12$ green. If the true number of green in a $90$-counter bag is $30$, how does the estimate compare?
mastery.a: estimate $36$, which is $6$ above the true $30$

mastery.q: A jar of $120$ marbles is sampled $40$ times: $10$ red, $14$ blue, $16$ green. Estimate the number green.
Over $25$ draws, red appeared $15$ times. Estimate how many draws out of the next $100$ would be red.
Cards drawn with replacement $50$ times gave $20$ hearts. A pile has $75$ cards. Estimate the number of hearts.
A sample of $200$ draws gave $50$ blue from a tub of $400$. Estimate the blue in the tub, then state $P(\text{blue})$ as a fraction.

Two samples from one bag of $60$: sample A ($20$ draws) gives $\tfrac{1}{4}$ red, sample B ($60$ draws) gives $\tfrac{1}{3}$ red. Which sample's estimate of red counters should you trust more, and what is it?
A weather record of $40$ days had $24$ sunny. Estimate the sunny days in a year ($365$), rounded to the nearest whole.
